summary: DHH endorses Alan Cooper's insight that code itself is not a company's true asset — relationships, customer knowledge, and institutional expertise are what matter. He uses his own experience at a previous company to illustrate the point, describing how veteran programmers were allowed to leave without preserving their knowledge. The company prioritized shipping code for imaginary deadlines over retaining people, quality, or documentation. The result was massive loss of human resource investment.
key_points: Code is not an asset — relationships with customers and vendors, plus institutional knowledge, are the real value
Companies that fail to create knowledge trails when experienced developers leave destroy their own investments
Obsessing over release dates at the expense of people, quality, and documentation is self-defeating
money_quotes: Code is not an asset... it's your relationships with your customers and vendors and the knowledge of your people that are valuable
They let one veteran programmer after another leave without any knowledge trail for the rest to pick up. Human resource investments lost en masse.
It was always about the code. About getting it ready for another imaginary release date -- everything else expendable (people, quality, documentation).
key_insight: Companies that treat code as their primary asset while letting experienced people walk out the door are destroying the very knowledge and relationships that actually create value.
